content.text: Why Search Is the First External Tool

An LLM's training data has a knowledge cutoff and contains no private or real-time information. Connecting a search tool closes both gaps: the model can retrieve current events via a web search API and private knowledge via a vector search over your own documents. Search is typically the first external tool wired into any LLM application.
There are three distinct search patterns you will use:
— query a public search engine (Tavily, Brave, Bing, SerpAPI) for current, publicly-available information.
— embed the query and retrieve nearest-neighbour chunks from a vector store (your private documents, product catalog, knowledge base).
— run a BM25-style search against a structured index (Elasticsearch, database FTS) for exact-term matching.
A search tool wraps the API call, normalises the result into a string the LLM can read, and returns it. The LLM never sees raw HTTP — it sees text.
Tavily is a search API designed for LLM use: it returns clean, readable snippets rather than raw HTML, and supports filtering by recency and domain. It is the default in LangChain's

# pip install tavily-python
from tavily import TavilyClient

client = TavilyClient(api_key="tvly-...")   # set TAVILY_API_KEY env var in production

def web_search(query: str, max_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    """Return a list of {title, url, content} dicts."""
    response = client.search(
        query=query,
        max_results=max_results,
        search_depth="basic",   # "advanced" for deeper extraction
    )
    return [
        {
            "title": r["title"],
            "url": r["url"],
            "content": r["content"],
        }
        for r in response["results"]
    ]

results = web_search("LangChain latest release notes")
for r in results:
    print(r["title"], r["url"])
Wrapping this as an OpenAI function-calling tool:
LangChain provides ready-made wrappers for the most common search APIs. They implement the
interface so they drop into any LangChain agent without custom code.

from langchain_community.tools.tavily_search import TavilySearchResults
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
search = TavilySearchResults(max_results=5)

# create_react_agent wires the ReAct loop automatically
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools=[search])

result = agent.invoke({
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What happened in AI news this week?"}]
})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)

Vector search retrieves documents by meaning rather than keywords. The query is embedded into the same vector space as the stored documents, and the nearest neighbours are returned. This is the backbone of RAG over private document collections.

# Using Chroma as the vector store
# pip install langchain-chroma langchain-openai chromadb

from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Create and populate the vector store
db = Chroma(
    collection_name="company_docs",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
)

# Index some documents (do this once during ingestion)
docs = [
    Document(page_content="Our return policy: items can be returned within 30 days.", metadata={"source": "policy.pdf"}),
    Document(page_content="Shipping takes 3-5 business days for standard delivery.", metadata={"source": "shipping.pdf"}),
    Document(page_content="Premium members get free overnight shipping on all orders.", metadata={"source": "premium.pdf"}),
]
db.add_documents(docs)

@tool
def search_company_docs(query: str, k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Search internal company documentation for policies, procedures, and product info.
    Use when the user asks about company-specific information such as returns,
    shipping, pricing, or account details. Do NOT use for general web questions.
    """
    results = db.similarity_search(query, k=k)
    if not results:
        return "No relevant documents found."
    parts = []
    for doc in results:
        source = doc.metadata.get("source", "unknown")
        parts.append(f"[{source}]\n{doc.page_content}")
    return "\n\n".join(parts)

# Use with a LangChain agent
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools=[search_company_docs])

result = agent.invoke({
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Can I return a product after 3 weeks?"}]
})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)
Hybrid Search — Combining Keyword and Vector

Keyword search (BM25) finds exact terms; vector search finds semantic matches. Combining both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) outperforms either alone — especially for proper nouns, product codes, and domain jargon that embeddings may not handle well.

from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain.retrievers import EnsembleRetriever
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_core.documents import Document

docs = [
    Document(page_content="Invoice INV-2025-0042 from Acme Corp, total $8,154 USD."),
    Document(page_content="Our refund policy allows returns within 30 days of purchase."),
    Document(page_content="The P100 GPU supports CUDA 11.x and has 16 GB HBM2 memory."),
]

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vector_store = Chroma.from_documents(docs, embeddings)
vector_retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3})

bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_documents(docs)
bm25_retriever.k = 3

# EnsembleRetriever uses RRF to merge results; weights control influence
hybrid_retriever = EnsembleRetriever(
    retrievers=[bm25_retriever, vector_retriever],
    weights=[0.5, 0.5],
)

@tool
def hybrid_search(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Search the document store using both keyword and semantic matching.
    Returns the most relevant documents. Use for questions about invoices,
    product specs, policies, or any company records.
    """
    results = hybrid_retriever.invoke(query)
    return "\n\n".join(doc.page_content for doc in results)
The LLM only reads what you return from the tool. Poor formatting leads to poor synthesis. Match the format to the use case:
Numbered list with title, URL, and snippet per result
Include URL so LLM can cite sources
Source filename + chunk content, separated by blank lines
Use when the LLM needs to extract specific fields
Top-k only; summarise or truncate remaining
Keep total under ~4k tokens per tool result
A search tool that works poorly in isolation will not improve with a better LLM. Validate each item before wiring the tool into an agent:
say "Use when user asks about recent news or live data" — not "Search the web".
if you have both a web search and a doc search tool, the description of each must say explicitly when NOT to use it.
truncate or summarise to keep tool results under ~4k tokens. Oversized results push earlier conversation out of context.
tool results must always be strings. Catch exceptions and return {"error": "...message..."} as JSON rather than raising.
strip HTML before returning. The LLM wastes tokens on tags and may mis-read them.
always include source URLs or document names. The LLM can then attribute its answer.
Search tools are the LLM's connection to the outside world.

A well-designed search tool with a clear description and clean output will be called at the right time and will produce grounded, verifiable answers. Invest in formatting the result — the LLM only synthesises what you give it.
